Article Name: Here's your first look at Google's upcoming 'Proactive Assistance' feature for Gemini
Article Text: Google is steadily expanding Gemini’s role across your phone, with a clear push toward making it more context-aware and deeply integrated into your daily usage. Earlier this year, the company rolled out Personal Intelligence, a feature that taps into apps like Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and Search to deliver more relevant suggestions and tailored responses. Now, it looks like Google is working on taking things a step further with a new “Proactive Assistance” feature in Gemini that can surface helpful suggestions based on what you’re doing, even before you think to ask. We spotted Proactive Assistance while digging through the Google app (version 17.18.22.sa.arm64), where we also managed to enable the feature. While not yet publicly available, the screenshots we managed to grab give us a clear idea of how Proactive Assistance will work once Google rolls it out. Proactive Assistance lives inside the Gemini settings menu, where users can toggle it on or off and control which apps feed into it. From what we’ve seen so far, you can turn the feature on/off with a single switch. According to the in-app description, Proactive Assistance pulls from three main sources, including what’s currently on your screen, your notifications, and the data from apps you explicitly allow. The goal seems to be to deliver timely, relevant suggestions based on what you’re doing. These could include things like reminders, contextual actions, or quick insights that appear right when they’re useful, not after you go looking for them. A Connected apps section lets you decide which data sources Gemini can use for the feature. Currently, the supported apps include Contacts and Messages. However, additional integrations like Gmail and Calendar fall under “Personal Intelligence apps,” which links to the broader connected apps dashboard. Google is also keeping user privacy in mind and notes that all data used by the Proactive Assistance feature is processed entirely on-device, with information stored in a private, encrypted environment. There’s no word yet on when Google plans to roll this out publicly, but given how fleshed out the feature already is, it may not be far off.
GPT Summary: Google is developing a new feature called "Proactive Assistance" for Gemini, aiming to enhance its context-awareness and integration into daily phone usage. Building on its earlier "Personal Intelligence" feature, Proactive Assistance is designed to offer timely, relevant suggestions based on your current activities without requiring you to ask. Accessible through the Gemini settings, users can toggle the feature on or off and control the apps contributing data to it. The feature relies on information from the screen, notifications, and apps that users permit. Initial integration includes Contacts and Messages, with broader connections to Personal Intelligence apps like Gmail and Calendar. Emphasizing user privacy, Google ensures that all data associated with Proactive Assistance is processed on-device and stored in an encrypted environment. While the feature isn't publicly available yet, its current development hints that a release may be imminent.
Gemini Summary: Google is preparing to launch "Proactive Assistance," a new feature for Gemini designed to offer timely, context-aware suggestions even before users think to ask. Building on Gemini's existing "Personal Intelligence," this feature will integrate with what's currently on your screen, your notifications, and data from explicitly allowed apps like Contacts and Messages to provide relevant reminders, actions, or insights. Users will have control over toggling the feature on/off and selecting which apps feed data, with Google emphasizing privacy by processing all data on-device within an encrypted environment. While not yet publicly available, the feature appears well-developed, suggesting a rollout may be imminent.

The feature pulls information from three sources — what’s on your screen, your notifications, and data from apps you explicitly allow (currently including Contacts and Messages, with Gmail and Calendar tied to the existing Personal Intelligence system). Users will be able to toggle the feature on or off directly in Gemini’s settings and control which apps can feed it data. All processing happens entirely on-device in a private, encrypted environment to protect user privacy. Although the feature is not yet publicly available, it appears largely complete, suggesting a rollout could be coming soon.
